subject: Oncology
title: Oncologic emergencies
preambleMarkdown:
# Oncologic emergencies

> **Clinical-review notice:** This is a bullet-first examination and clinical-orientation draft. Local protocols, medication doses, procedural thresholds, pregnancy/pediatric pathways, emergency workflows, and jurisdiction-specific guidance must be checked before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Oncologic emergencies is a neoplastic, premalignant, or tumor-related topic in oncology.
- This article focuses on tumor biology, staging, histology, treatment intent, oncologic emergencies, survivorship, and palliation.
- The practical clinical priority is malignant potential, staging, treatment intent, and complications.
- Start by deciding whether the presentation is stable, time-sensitive, complicated, recurrent, or incidental.
- Imported source notes are preserved below for clinical review and citation reconciliation.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify unstable, time-sensitive, or function-threatening presentations first.
- Anchor the assessment of Oncologic emergencies to the patient context: age, pregnancy status when relevant, immune status, comorbid disease, medications, prior episodes, and local resources.
- Use focused history and examination before broad testing.
- Choose investigations that change diagnosis, severity assessment, treatment, disposition, or follow-up.
- Treat reversible danger early and give clear safety-net instructions.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Oncologic emergencies is best defined by the specific clinical problem, anatomic site, mechanism, severity, and care setting.
- For this topic, the core concept is malignant potential, staging, treatment intent, and complications.
- Separate suspected, confirmed, recurrent, complicated, and resolved disease states.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Benign, premalignant, in situ, localized invasive, regional, metastatic, recurrent, or treatment-related state.
- Histology, grade, stage, biomarkers, and performance status.
- Curative, disease-control, surveillance, symptom-relief, or end-of-life treatment intent.
- Screen-detected, symptomatic, incidental, familial, or therapy-associated presentation.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The epidemiology of Oncologic emergencies depends on age, sex, geography, exposure, comorbid disease, medication use, pregnancy status when relevant, and ascertainment setting.
- In oncology, exam questions usually test the common population plus the high-risk exception.
- Risk estimates should be updated from local data or current guideline sources before publication.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify the organ, tissue, vessel, nerve, compartment, joint, pathway, or system most directly involved in Oncologic emergencies.
- Map nearby structures that explain referred pain, spread, obstruction, ischemia, neurologic deficits, imaging findings, or procedure risk.
- For non-anatomic topics, focus on the structure most likely to change symptoms, testing, or treatment.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Define the normal function that Oncologic emergencies disrupts or depends on.
- Connect the physiology to symptoms, examination findings, laboratory abnormalities, imaging appearance, and treatment targets.
- Consider compensation, reserve, feedback loops, and decompensation triggers.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The central mechanism in Oncologic emergencies should explain why the patient develops the key symptoms and complications.
- Mechanisms may include obstruction, inflammation, ischemia, infection, immune injury, degeneration, trauma, thrombosis, endocrine imbalance, toxicity, neoplasia, or functional dysregulation.
- Distinguish the primary process from secondary complications and treatment-related harm.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider the common causes of Oncologic emergencies first, then actively check for dangerous but less common causes.
- Review congenital, acquired, infectious, inflammatory, traumatic, metabolic, neoplastic, medication-related, toxic, pregnancy-related, immune, and idiopathic causes when relevant.
- Use the time course and exposure history to narrow the cause.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Risk changes with age, family history, baseline disease, immune status, pregnancy, medications, procedures, travel, occupation, environment, and prior episodes.
- For Oncologic emergencies, risk factors should guide pretest probability and prevention, not replace objective assessment.
- Document modifiable risks that can be addressed during treatment or follow-up.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider inherited disease when presentation is early, familial, recurrent, syndromic, unusually severe, or associated with congenital findings.
- Ask about family history, ancestry when clinically relevant, consanguinity, pregnancy losses, and similar symptoms in relatives.
- Use genetic testing when results change diagnosis, screening, treatment, reproductive counseling, or family risk assessment.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider infection when fever, exposure, immunocompromise, procedure, device, wound, systemic inflammation, or rapid clinical decline is present.
- Identify the likely source, route of spread, organism group, need for cultures, need for isolation, and need for source control.
- Antimicrobial decisions should account for severity, host risk, allergies, renal or hepatic function, pregnancy status, local resistance, and prior cultures.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Mass effect, bleeding, obstruction, pain, weight loss, constitutional symptoms, paraneoplastic findings, or incidental screening abnormality may occur.
- Red flags depend on anatomic site and include neurologic compromise, airway compromise, uncontrolled bleeding, or sepsis.
- Treatment history matters because recurrence and treatment toxicity can mimic new disease.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask onset, duration, progression, triggers, exposures, associated symptoms, prior episodes, and treatments already tried.
- Review medications, allergies, procedures, devices, travel, occupation, sexual history when relevant, pregnancy status when relevant, family history, and baseline function.
- For Oncologic emergencies, ask targeted questions that separate uncomplicated disease from dangerous complications.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Start with vital signs, mental status, perfusion, respiratory effort, hydration, pain severity, and overall appearance.
- Perform a focused oncology examination that directly tests the suspected mechanism.
- Look for systemic involvement, complications, mimics, and findings that change urgency or disposition.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Shock, altered mental status, respiratory distress, severe hypoxemia, syncope, seizure, major bleeding, or rapidly progressive symptoms.
- Severe pain out of proportion, new neurologic deficit, threatened airway, sepsis, peritonitis, limb ischemia, compartment syndrome, or vision threat.
- Any Oncologic emergencies presentation with organ dysfunction, pregnancy risk, immunocompromise, failed initial treatment, or diagnostic uncertainty needs a lower threshold for escalation.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Compare Oncologic emergencies with disorders that share the same dominant symptom, anatomic site, exposure, laboratory pattern, or imaging abnormality.
- Prioritize dangerous mimics that would be harmed by delay or by the wrong treatment.
- Use acuity, location, risk factors, examination findings, labs, imaging, and response to treatment to narrow the differential.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- First decide whether Oncologic emergencies is unstable, complicated, recurrent, incidental, or safe for outpatient workup.
- Use the highest-yield initial test for the suspected syndrome rather than ordering broad panels without a decision point.
- If early tests are negative but suspicion remains high, reassess the history, repeat focused examination, and choose second-line testing based on the remaining differential.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use labs to assess severity, complications, treatment safety, and alternative diagnoses.
- Use imaging, bedside tests, cultures, tissue sampling, functional testing, or procedures when results change management.
- Interpret results with pretest probability and clinical context.
- Avoid tests that add risk, delay, or incidental findings without changing the next step.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnosis of Oncologic emergencies should combine compatible clinical pattern with accepted laboratory, imaging, procedural, histologic, physiologic, or scoring criteria when available.
- Do not diagnose from one nonspecific abnormality when the full syndrome does not fit.
- Document uncertainty and the follow-up plan when the diagnosis remains provisional.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Severity depends on stability, organ dysfunction, symptom burden, anatomic extent, complications, treatment risk, and ability to follow up.
- Identify who needs urgent referral, admission, ICU care, procedure, surgery, isolation, rehabilitation, or close outpatient review.
- Use validated disease-specific scoring or staging systems when they exist and are available locally.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm diagnosis with tissue when required, stage before definitive therapy when possible, and define treatment intent.
- Use multidisciplinary planning for surgery, radiation, systemic therapy, surveillance, fertility, rehabilitation, and palliation.
- Treat oncologic emergencies immediately while definitive cancer planning continues.

### Initial Management

- Confirm whether Oncologic emergencies is stable, unstable, incidental, recurrent, or complicated.
- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, mental status, pain, hydration, perfusion, and immediate threats.
- Escalate early when red flags, organ dysfunction, procedural need, or diagnostic uncertainty is present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, observation, rehabilitation, isolation, procedural planning, or environmental change when it addresses malignant potential, staging, treatment intent, and complications.
- Tailor activity, diet, wound, exposure, work, driving, return-to-play, or safety advice to the clinical setting.

### Pharmacological Management

- Use medication only when the indication is clear and contraindications have been checked.
- Verify dose, route, duration, interactions, allergies, renal and hepatic adjustment, pregnancy or lactation status, and monitoring.

### Procedures

- Use procedures when they confirm diagnosis, obtain tissue or fluid, provide source control, relieve obstruction, restore alignment, repair anatomy, or treat complications.
- Check consent, anatomy, imaging, bleeding risk, infection risk, sedation risk, equipment, and aftercare before proceeding.

### Surgical Management

- Surgical referral is needed for resectable disease, biopsy planning, obstruction, bleeding, perforation, cord compression, airway compromise, or palliation.
- Timing depends on severity, anatomy, operative risk, patient goals, and local expertise.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Track symptoms, vital signs, function, pain, intake/output when relevant, focused examination findings, key labs, adverse effects, and response to therapy.
- For Oncologic emergencies, monitoring should detect deterioration, treatment toxicity, recurrence, and missed complications.
- Escalate if expected improvement does not occur or new red flags appear.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm diagnosis, severity, treatment response, adherence, side effects, and patient understanding.
- Arrange repeat examination, labs, imaging, rehabilitation, surveillance, medication review, or specialty care when indicated.
- Give a clear reassessment interval and explicit return precautions.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Complications of Oncologic emergencies may be acute, chronic, recurrent, treatment-related, functional, psychosocial, or procedure-related.
- Prioritize complications that threaten life, airway, limb, vision, fertility, neurologic function, organ function, or public health.
- Document warning symptoms that require urgent reassessment.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Prognosis depends on cause, severity, timing of recognition, comorbid disease, age, treatment response, complications, and follow-up reliability.
- Early recognition of dangerous Oncologic emergencies patterns improves disposition and reduces preventable complications.
- Published prognostic statistics should be checked against current specialty sources before release.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Address modifiable risk factors, medication safety, vaccination or prophylaxis when relevant, exposure reduction, screening, and recurrence prevention.
- For Oncologic emergencies, prevention should target the cause, the complication pathway, and the follow-up failure points.
- Use patient-specific counseling rather than generic reassurance.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Explain Oncologic emergencies in plain language, including what is known, what remains uncertain, and what symptoms should trigger urgent care.
- Review medication purpose, expected benefit, side effects, duration, and what to do if symptoms worsen.
- Confirm the patient understands follow-up, restrictions, wound or device care when relevant, and return precautions.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Monitor vital signs, pain, function, mental status, intake/output when relevant, wounds or devices, and response to treatment.
- Escalate fever, bleeding, respiratory change, neurologic change, perfusion change, uncontrolled pain, medication reaction, or worsening function.
- Reinforce education, discharge instructions, safe mobility, adherence, and follow-up logistics.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy or lactation status, interactions, duplication, contraindications, adherence barriers, and monitoring needs.
- For Oncologic emergencies, medication review should include both treatment drugs and drugs that may cause, worsen, or mimic the problem.
- High-risk drugs require a documented indication, duration, monitoring plan, and counseling.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask about bleeding risk, anticoagulants, antiplatelets, immunosuppression, prosthetic material, active infection, and relevant medication adverse effects before invasive dental work.
- Coordinate with the treating clinician when medication interruption, prophylaxis, or infection control is being considered.
- Oncologic emergencies does not automatically imply dental antibiotic prophylaxis; use condition-specific indications and local guidance.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pediatrics: use age-specific normal values, developmental context, weight-based dosing, caregiver education, and safeguarding awareness.
- Pregnancy: stabilize the mother first, choose pregnancy-aware testing and medications when possible, and involve obstetrics for significant disease.
- Older adults: consider frailty, atypical presentation, falls, delirium, kidney function, polypharmacy, cognition, and goals of care.
- Renal or hepatic impairment: adjust diagnostic contrast, medications, dosing, and monitoring.
- Immunocompromised patients: lower the threshold for atypical infection, severe disease, cultures, imaging, admission, and interaction review.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current guidance for Oncologic emergencies may vary by country, specialty society, age group, pregnancy status, disease severity, and available resources.
- Compare local policy with major society guidance for diagnostic thresholds, treatment sequencing, drug choice, procedural timing, and follow-up intervals.
- Document where local practice differs from international guidance and why.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize first when the patient is unstable; diagnostic precision comes after immediate threats are controlled.
- Match each test to a decision it will change.
- Reassess after treatment because nonresponse is often a diagnostic clue.
- For Oncologic emergencies, the highest-yield clinical move is to identify severity and the next safe action.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Treating Oncologic emergencies as a label instead of confirming mechanism, severity, and complications.
- Ordering broad tests without a focused question.
- Missing red flags because the initial symptom sounds common.
- Ignoring pregnancy status, renal function, hepatic function, allergies, interactions, or follow-up barriers.
- Discharging without clear return precautions.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Step 1: know the mechanism, anatomy, microbiology, pharmacology, or physiology behind the topic.
- Step 2 CK: know the next best diagnostic or management step.
- Step 3: know disposition, monitoring, complications, follow-up, and patient safety.
- Board exams: know contraindications, high-risk exceptions, and when to escalate.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnosis and staging are separate steps.
- Treatment depends on stage, histology, biomarkers, performance status, comorbidity, and patient goals.
- Know emergency presentations: cord compression, tumor lysis, febrile neutropenia, airway compromise, and superior vena cava syndrome.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- This Oncologic emergencies draft still needs specialty review before publication.
- Medication doses, procedural thresholds, screening intervals, risk scores, and guideline claims should be localized.
- Imported notes, when present, should be reconciled with current evidence and cited sources.

sections.heading: Imported Source Notes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
<!-- source: step-up-emergency-medicine import start -->
Source: Step-Up to Emergency Medicine. Imported on 2026-07-14.

### Source Excerpt: Oncologic Emergencies

_Source lines: 32284-32676._

```text
III. ONCOLOGIC EMERGENCIES
A. Discussion. Emergencies attributed to cancer can be classified as biochemical,
immunologic, or mechanical.

1. Biochemical derangements from cancer may involve electrolyte, endocrine, or
rheologic vascular dysfunction.

a. Hypercalcemia occurs with renal cell carcinoma; multiple myeloma; bone
metastases from breast, prostate, or lung primaries; and lymphoma.

b. Syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone (SIADH) secretion may be
seen in patients with intracerebral, intrathoracic, and certain abdominal-
pelvic tumors.

c. Hyperviscosity syndrome occurs in patients with Waldenstrom macroglobu-
linemia, chronic myelocytic leukemia, and multiple myeloma.

d. Adrenal insufficiency and shock may be seen in patients in whom chronic
steroid replacement is withdrawn or in those with lung or breast carcino-
mas, malignant melanoma, or retroperitoneal malignancy.

e. Tumor lysis syndrome is usually anticipated and treated prophylactically by
oncologists but may be seen in patients presenting to the ED. This entity
is most commonly seen in patients with leukemia or lymphoma and is
characteristically seen 1 to 5 days after chemotherapy or radiotherapy of
sensitive and fast-growing tumors. Acute cell lysis occurs, with release of
potassium, nucleic acids, and phosphates. Multiple organ failure and death
can result.

2. Immunologic complications of cancer are partially caused by cancer-mediated
immunosuppression, but they primarily result from chemotherapy or radiation
therapy.

a. Myelosuppression, with resultant granulocytopenia and sepsis, is a primary
concern in patients with cancer. Opportunistic infection is also a significant
risk in the immunosuppressed patient.

b. Immune-mediated thrombocytopenia can be seen.

c. Suppression of marrow function can result in anemia.

Comparing Coagulation Disorders and Their Effects	TABLE 16-1

PT, prothrombin time; PTT, partial thromboplastin time; DIC, disseminated intravascular coagulation.

396 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Hematologic and Oncologic Emergencies
d. Overwhelming infection, which may occur with opportunistic microbes, is
a major cause of death in patients with cancer.

3. Mechanical complications of tumor growth may be the first manifestations of
cancer, and they often threaten life or limb (see Clinical Pearl 16-4).

B. Clinical features
1. Biochemical derangements
a. Hypercalcemia, occurring in up to 40% of patients with multiple myeloma
and also commonly seen in multiple squamous cancers (especially squa-
mous of lung). May cause back pain, constipation, or altered mental
status
b. SIADH is primarily a laboratory diagnosis in patients presenting with inap-
propriate free water retention.

c. Hyperviscosity syndromes occur when the serum viscosity is greater than
five times that of water, and it may be seen when the hematocrit increases
beyond 50% to 60%. A leukocrit exceeding 10% may also be associated with
significant hyperviscosity.

i. Fatigue, headache, anorexia, and somnolence are early nonspecific
symptoms of hyperviscosity.

ii. Localized neurologic deficits, with possible progression to coma, may
develop as microthromboses develop in the central nervous system.

d. Adrenal insufficiency, like hyperviscosity, can manifest as altered mental
status. Potentially life-threatening cardiovascular involvement with adrenal
shock is possible.

e. Tumor lysis syndrome can be characterized by multiple organ failure and
death.

2. Immunologic complications. Features of thrombocytopenia (from immune-
mediated platelet destruction) and anemia in patients with cancer are similar
to presenting signs and symptoms of these disorders in other patients.

3. Mechanical complications
a. Airway obstruction may be indicated by dyspnea, stridor, nasal flaring, or
wheezing.

b. Malignant effusions accumulate over long periods of time and, therefore,
may reach significant size (sometimes greater than 500 mL) before causing
symptoms characteristic of pericardial fluid accumulation.

c. Superior vena cava syndrome results from tumor restriction of blood flow
in the superior vena cava (Figure 16-2). Blood flow obstruction results in
elevated venous pressures in the areas drained by the superior vena cava
(i.e., arms, neck, face, cerebrum). Symptoms reflect the anatomic distribu-
tion of the vein's drainage and include headache, facial or arm edema, and
fullness in the neck and face. As the disease progresses, an increase in ICP
and syncope may be noted.

Quick 	HIT
Hypercalcemia often causes
stones (kidney/biliary),
bones (bone pain), groans/
moans (constipation or
abdominal pain), and psychi-
atric moans (mood swings).

Complications of Tumor Growth
1. Airway obstruction may be seen in patients with tumors of the ears, nose, or throat; lymphoma; and
metastatic lung carcinoma. Tumor impingement may occur at all levels of the respiratory tract, with upper
respiratory tract, ear, nose, and throat tumors causing proximal obstruction and endobronchial lesions
restricting airflow distally.

2. Malignant pericardial effusion and tamponade may be seen after radiation therapy or in patients
with breast, ovary, or lung carcinoma. In addition, acute leukemia, Hodgkin lymphoma, or melanoma may
present with pericardial disease.

3. Superior vena cava syndrome is seen in patients with lymphoma or with oat-cell or squamous-cell lung
carcinomas.

4. Spinal cord compression may be the first sign of neoplasm, and it is seen in patients with multiple
myeloma; lymphomas; and carcinoma of the lung, breast, or prostate.

PEARL	CLINICAL 	16-4
H E M A T O L O G I C A N D O N C O L O G I C E M E R G E N C I E S 	 	397

Hematologic and Oncologic Emergencies
d. Spinal cord compression may present as paraparesis, hypoesthesia, or gait
disturbance. Paraplegia, marked sensory deficits, urinary incontinence or
retention, and loss of rectal sphincter tone usually indicate a more advanced
process.

4. Signs and symptoms of other concurrent diseases. Concurrent disorders occur
in patients with cancer with increased frequency because of the malignancy, can-
cer therapy, or the patient's generalized debilitated state. These include coronary
artery disease, hypercoagulability and thrombosis (e.g., Trousseau syndrome
of visceral malignancy), intravascular volume depletion; rapidly progressive
renal failure from glomerular amyloid deposition may be seen in patients with
multiple myeloma or lymphoma; and occult bleeding in cancer patients may be
caused by intra-arterial chemotherapy or vomiting (e.g. Mallory-Weiss tears).

C. Differential diagnosis. The long list of tumor-related emergencies implies a simi-
larly extensive list of differential diagnoses. Because oncologic emergencies may
affect blood chemistries, organ function, airway patency, and perfusion, the poten-
tial of cancer-related illness should be considered in any patient who presents to
the ED.

D. Evaluation
1. Biochemical derangements
a. Hypercalcemia of malignancy represents a relatively easy diagnostic entity.

Calcium levels are readily measured by the hospital laboratory.

i. Ionized calcium levels should be interpreted in light of pH and serum
albumin. Parathyroid hormone levels should be measured.

ii. An electrocardiogram may demonstrate a shortened QT interval in
patients with hypercalcemia.

b. SIADH is evaluated by laboratory analysis of serum and urine sodium and
urine-specific gravity. Physical examination clues may point toward intrace-
rebral or intrapulmonary processes in patients with SIADH and no known
tumor.

i. The primary abnormalities in SIADH are hyponatremia and urine that
contains a high sodium level (greater than 30 mEq/L) and is not opti-
mally concentrated.

ii. Normal renal, adrenal, and thyroid function must be demonstrated
before the diagnosis of SIADH is established.

c. Hyperviscosity syndromes may be difficult to diagnose clinically because
presenting symptoms can be vague. Laboratory analysis is usually diagnostic.

i. A CBC reveals elevated hematocrit and microscopic Rouleau formation.

Sometimes, the clue to the diagnosis of hyperviscosity comes with labo-
ratory reporting that the blood is "too thick" to run tests.

ii. Neurologic examination may reveal focal deficits.

F I G U R E
16-2 	Patient with superior vena cava syndrome.

(Courtesy of Lawrence B. Stack, MD.)

398 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Hematologic and Oncologic Emergencies
iii. Funduscopy should be performed to search for "sausage-link" retinal
vasculature, hemorrhages, or exudates.

d. Adrenal insufficiency is suspected based on either electrolyte analysis or the
clinical appearance of a patient with potential adrenal dysfunction. Because
treatment is empiric, response to therapy may aid in making the diagnosis.

i. Primary laboratory abnormalities are hypoglycemia, hyponatremia,
hyperkalemia, and eosinophilia.

ii. Physical examination for cardiovascular stability is important in patients
with adrenal insufficiency; hypotension causes hemodynamic collapse.

e. Tumor lysis syndrome. The diagnosis may be made based on the history
and recent cancer therapy or steroids. Some of these patients may not have
a known history of neoplasm. Laboratory analysis helps make the definitive
diagnosis.

i. Hyperkalemia occurring in tumor lysis syndrome may also cause peaked
T-wave electrocardiogram changes and prolonged QRS complexes.

ii. Hyperuricemia can be found in patients with tumor lysis. Uric acid
levels should be checked on all patients who are candidates for this
diagnosis.

iii. Hyperphosphatemia may cause dangerous hypocalcemia.

2. Immunologic complications
a. Infection. The frequency and severity of infection increase with absolute
neutrophil count lower than 1000/mm3
. Evaluation of febrile neutropenic
patients involves empiric antibiotics, radiography, body fluid analysis, and
culturing to detect the site of infection.

b. Immune-mediated thrombocytopenia may be suggested by the presence of
petechiae on physical examination.

c. Anemia. Patients report fatigue, and the physical examination may reveal
skin or palpebral conjunctival pallor. The CBC is diagnostic, but optimal
interpretation requires comparison with a known hemoglobin baseline.

3. Mechanical complications
a. Airway obstruction. Portable chest radiography is usually performed be-
cause of potential patient instability. Fiberoptic laryngoscopy may be used
for airway visualization.

b. Malignant pericardial effusion and tamponade present with similar physical
findings as do other causes of pericardial tamponade. Beck triad of hypoten-
sion, venous distention, and muffled heart tones is a late finding.

i. Echocardiography is the best tool for evaluation of pericardial fluid but
may not be available in the ED.

ii. Pericardiocentesis is indicated in patients with hemodynamic compro-
mise and is both diagnostic and therapeutic.

c. Superior vena cava syndrome, in its advanced stages, may be associated
with papilledema caused by increased ICP.

i. Early findings are neck and upper thoracic venous distention, facial
plethora and telangiectasia, and mild edema of the face and arms.

ii. A palpable supraclavicular mass may be identified.

iii. A chest radiograph often demonstrates mediastinal or lung abnormality.

d. Spinal cord compression may present as acute urinary retention. Patients
with this or any other neurologic complaint suggestive of cord compression
(e.g., loss of perirectal sensation) should undergo examination of reflexes,
gait, sensorimotor function, and sphincter tone to further evaluate for signs
of acute cord compression. A sensory level or distal flaccid paralysis may be
seen. Patients with suspected cord compression require further consultation,
often including emergent CT scan, magnetic resonance imaging, or myelo-
graphic imaging.

E. Therapy
1. Biochemical derangements
a. Hypercalcemia associated with cancer usually improves with saline infusion
and possibly intravenous furosemide. Hemodialysis or peritoneal dialysis is
Quick 	HIT
In tumor lysis syndrome,
hyperkalemia can cause
cardiac derangements and
should be treated immedi-
ately, although other meta-
bolic abnormalities can also
be life-threatening.

H E M A T O L O G I C A N D O N C O L O G I C E M E R G E N C I E S 	 	399

Hematologic and Oncologic Emergencies
reserved for patients who do not respond to more conservative measures.

Bisphosphonates and glucocorticoids are usually initiated in the inpatient
setting.

b. SIADH. Treatment focuses on free water restriction and, in cases of cardiac
or neurologic toxicity, includes infusion of 100 to 250 mL of 3% saline
solution.

c. Symptomatic hyperviscosity syndrome in patients with a hematocrit greater
than 60% is treated with hydration and phlebotomy (of 1 to 2 U) with
saline and RBC replacement.

d. Adrenal insufficiency and shock. Primary therapy is the administration of
intravenous hydrocortisone along with volume resuscitation. This dose may
be repeated every 6 to 8 hours. Occasionally, higher doses or pressors may
be necessary. If the diagnosis of adrenal insufficiency is unclear, treatment
can proceed using dexamethasone while performing an adrenocorticotropic
hormone stimulation test.

e. Tumor lysis syndrome. Ideally, therapy is preventive, but the ED physician
can effectively address many of the abnormalities resulting from lysis.

i. Vigorous hydration, with careful attention to renal function, is a cor-
nerstone of tumor lysis syndrome treatment.

ii. Urinary alkalinization (to pH greater than 7) and allopurinol are recom-
mended to address increased uric acid levels caused by tumor lysis.

iii. Careful monitoring of calcium levels is important, especially for
patients receiving bicarbonate for urinary alkalinization.

2. Immunologic complications
a. Infection. Antibiotic therapy should be tailored toward any known sources of
infection based on physical exam and laboratory evaluation. Without a clear
source of infection, broad-spectrum antibiotics should be administered with
the primary goal to administer appropriate antibiotics as early as possible.

b. Immune-mediated thrombocytopenia may require transfusion, but trans-
fused platelets also are likely susceptible to destruction. Hematology consul-
tation is recommended before platelet transfusion in these patients.

c. Indications for transfusion in anemia are not significantly different in
patients with cancer as compared with other individuals.

3. Mechanical complications
a. Airway obstruction is treated by establishment of a secure airway. Those
patients with true airway emergencies require intubation in the ED.

Other oncologic patients with airway compromise may require surgical
tracheostomy.

b. Malignant pericardial effusion and tamponade are treated with fluid resusci-
tation and pericardiocentesis.

c. Superior vena cava syndrome, when accompanied by papilledema, should
be treated with furosemide and methylprednisolone to reduce the ICP pend-
ing definitive mediastinal radiotherapy.

d. Spinal cord compression is a true emergency, and it is amenable to rapid
therapy in the ED. Patients should receive dexamethasone while awaiting
definitive radiographic, radiotherapeutic, or operative intervention. Emergency
surgical decompression or radiotherapy can prevent irreversible neurologic
impairment.

4. Concurrent disorders. Patients with cancer are known to have increased fre-
quency of thrombotic disease, but the dangers of anticoagulation in this group
may prompt mechanical (i.e., filter placement) rather than pharmacologic
therapy. Early nephrology consultation is indicated in patients with tumor
lysis syndrome and potential renal insufficiency because hemodialysis may
be helpful.

F. 	Disposition. Admission is the rule for patients with oncologic emergencies.

400 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Traumatic Emergencies
401
17
William Gossman
TRAUMATIC
EMERGENCIES
I. INTRODUCTION
Trauma is the leading cause of mortality in the first four decades of life. It accounts for
approximately 60 to 80 million injuries and 10 million disabilities per year.
```
